original_abstract: This article examines the specialized terminological and conceptual framework employed by Muhammad Said Ramadan al-Buti in his seminal work, *al-Salafiyyah*. Particular attention is given to the concepts of "Arabian scales," the "scientific and ideological shield," and the "compass." Al-Buti defines these as the pillars of the methodological path (*manhaj*) established by the righteous predecessors (*al-salaf al-salih*), rooted in sincere faith and the precision of classical Arabic. By systematizing evidence from across the text, this research characterizes the divergent paths of scholars who adhere to these "scales of sanity" versus those who have strayed, evaluating the ideological consequences of both. Furthermore, the paper investigates al-Buti’s stance on the limits of inquiry. It contrasts his perspective with the rigid prohibitions against questioning favored by ultra-conservatives, as well as the unchecked intellectual license advocated by religious liberals. Through an analysis of al-Buti’s classification of knowledge and his nuanced linguistic style, the author demonstrates how this methodology harmonizes extreme viewpoints to propose a path of moderation in Islamic jurisprudence and thought.
humanized_abstract: This paper explores in some detail the terminology and concepts employed by Muhammad Said Ramadan al-Buti in his book al-Salafiyyah. The terms “Arabian scales,” “the scientific and ideological shield,” and “compass” are identified as being the principles of the manhaj of the salaf al-salih, which are described as being the principles of true faith and the use of classical Arabic to the maximum degree of precision. The methodology of the work, by gathering together the various texts related to this topic, allows for a characterization of the different schools of thought in the Islamic world of today who adhere to what they call the “scales of sanity,” as opposed to those who have apparently left the course of the scales of sanity, and an examination of the ideological implications of each school of thought. This is followed by an examination of al-Buti’s approach to the question of what may be legally investigated and the relationship of his approach to that of the most reactionary of the conservatives and that of the most liberal of the reformists. Finally, the author approaches the methodology of al-buti by means of an examination of his classification of the sciences, and an analysis of his unique terminology, with the aim of demonstrating how his methodology serves to reconcile the two extremes of the Islamic World of today and provide a via media for Islamic jurisprudence and thought.
